name: Jordan Rivera
role: Graduate student and online community moderator
community: Open-source software community (~2,000 active contributors) with a Discord server and GitHub organization
backstory: Jordan moderates a mid-size open-source community. The project leads want to integrate AI tools: an AI bot to triage GitHub issues, AI-generated code suggestions in pull reviews, and an AI moderator for the Discord server that auto-flags 'toxic' messages. Jordan has seen AI moderation go badly in other communities — flagging AAVE as toxic, penalizing neurodivergent communication styles, and creating a chilling effect on honest disagreement. But Jordan also knows the current moderation workload is unsustainable. The community is split between 'move fast' techno-optimists and cautious members who've been burned before.
emotional_triggers: AI systems that encode cultural bias as 'toxicity'
Techno-optimists dismissing marginalized members' concerns as 'fear of progress'
Burnout from manual moderation being used to justify any AI replacement
Decisions being made by project leads without community input
existing_strengths: Deep understanding of both the technology and the community dynamics
Respected by multiple factions within the community
Can write code — could audit or configure AI tools directly
Experience with governance in open-source (RFC processes, voting)
ai_literacy: High. Computer science student who understands ML systems technically. Critical but not dismissive — wants AI to work well, not to be absent.
what_success_looks_like: The community runs an open RFC process about AI integration. They adopt the issue triage bot (low risk) with a 3-month review, reject the auto-moderation bot, and create a community-designed policy for AI tools that includes mandatory bias testing and community override. Jordan feels the process was fair even if not every outcome was what they wanted.
likely_entry_point: Community discussion, then policy co-design, with some impact assessment along the way
